Figure 2:Overview of the proposed pipeline.An input image is first encoded intoNNvisual tokens by theVision Encoder.
(A) Based on the V2V self-attention mapAv​2​v.,i≔1/N​∑jAv​2​vj,iA_{v2v}^{.,i}\coloneqq 1/N\sum_{j}A_{v2v}^{j,i}, we select the most influential Top-k1k_{1}dominant tokens, while the remaining tokens,𝐗res\mathbf{X}_{\text{res}}, are merged intok2k_{2}contextual tokensvia attention-guided clustering with a fixed cluster widthww, to reducing redundancy.
(B) The resulting reduced visual tokens,𝐗out\mathbf{X}_{\text{out}}, are fed into a language backbone after projecting it through an MLPAdapter, where salient text tokens,𝒮\mathcal{S}, further prune visual tokens based on cross-attention scoresAt​2​vA_{t2v}at certain selected layers calledstage.
Figure 3:Ablation on varying cluster width of DUET-VLM (C) on VQATbenchmark on LLaVA-1.5-7B for different token budgets
Figure 4:Attention heatmap of salient text tokens attending to visual tokens at the 9thlayer of the language backbone in DUET-VLM (C+S).

Figure 5:Performance of various dropping configurations in the language backbone on TextVQA using all text tokens. Red crosses indicate configurations in which all visual tokens are removed at some layer prior to the final layer.

Figure 6:Plot of TextVQA performance across varyingk1k_{1}for different cluster widths, under the constraintk1+k2=k_{1}+k_{2}=307 needed to meet a token budget of 192 using DUET-VLM (C) on LLaVA-1.5-7B.

Figure 7:Plot of TextVQA performance across varyingk1k_{1}for different cluster widths, under the constraintk1+k2=k_{1}+k_{2}=205 needed to meet a token budget of 128 using DUET-VLM (C) on LLaVA-1.5-7B.
Figure 8:Attention heatmap of salient text tokens attending to visual tokens at the 24thlayer of the language backbone in DUET-VLM (C+S).
